text: from the Division of Mentally Invasive Surgery, in the Department of Surgery at Houston Methodist Hospital, and MITEI, the Methodist Institute for Technology, Innovation, and Education. Our patient was a female in the sixth decade of life with a known diagnosis of gallstones. She presented to an outside facility with right upper quadrant and right-sided pain which radiated both through to the back and to the right subscapular region. The patient underwent an abdominal ultrasound, an abdominal CT scan, and an abdominal MRI at the outside facility. Her outside physicians, based upon a review of her studies, referred her to our practice with the diagnosis of a type I choledochal cyst. The patient specifically requested a laparoscopic approach to her surgical care. Prior to her visit with us, she was referred by us to a senior gastroenterologist at our facility for an ERCP to better define her ductal anatomy. An endoscopic ultrasound was performed at the same time. Her endoscopic ultrasound showed shadowing gallstones filling the common bile duct all the way down to the ampulla. Her ERCP showed that she did not have a type I choledochal cyst. The fusiform shape of her common bile duct was due to its being filled with gallstones that tapered into the ampulla. The cystic duct and gallbladder were not visualized. A biliary stent was placed. She was consented and scheduled for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and removal of stones.

The surgeon observes a markedly contracted and distorted gallbladder with Hartman's pouch tightly packed with gallstones. Dense adhesions are present inferiorly and medially. The gallbladder is opened laterally to Hartman's pouch, revealing numerous large gallstones, which are removed and placed into a plastic specimen retrieval bag. Hartman's pouch is opened inferiorly, and additional gallstones are extracted and placed into another specimen retrieval bag. The gallbladder is irrigated to remove debris.

The surgeon identifies a mucosal line opening on the posterior wall, measuring over five millimeters in diameter, which appears to be a major bile duct from Segment V draining into the gallbladder bed. A three-milliliter choledochoscope is inserted into the opening, confirming it as a cholecystohepatic duct. The choledochoscope is then withdrawn and directed distally into Hartman's pouch, where the previously placed stent is visible at the ampulla of Vater. The scope is advanced through the ampulla, pushing stone fragments and debris into the duodenum, before being withdrawn.

The surgeon visualizes Hartman's pouch, identifying Moritzi syndrome type IIb, with a fistula occupying over fifty percent of the common bile duct diameter. A cholecystohepatic duct is also present. The distal gallbladder is excised and removed, and a Roux-en-Y cholecystogeogenostomy is planned for drainage.

The surgeon dissects lower midline adhesions to gain access for creating a Roux limb. A standard Roux-en-Y jejunostomy is constructed, featuring a 40 cm biliopancreatic limb, a 60 cm Roux limb, and a 2.5 cm stapled side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy. Sutures are applied to close the common enterotomy.

A window is created through the transverse mesocolon just anterior to the duodenum, and the Roux limb is passed cranially into the operative field. A posterior row of running 3-0 polydioxanone suture is placed from caudal to cranial, and the bowel is opened for anastomosis using a 3mm hook cautery. An anterior row of running 3-0 polydioxanone suture is then placed, and the two suture lines are tied together to complete the anastomosis. The Roux limb is secured to the gallbladder bed with a 3-0 polyglactin suture to prevent rotation and tension.
A 15 French fluted silastic drain is placed through the operative field, marking the conclusion of the operation.

An ERCP done at the time of her post-operative removal of her biliary stent shows a patent anastomosis and good filling of the biliary tree. The roux limb could not be filled with enough pressure to delineate the cholecystohepatic duct. Cholecystohepatic duct is a very rare bile duct anomaly with a frequency of zero point seven percent to one point two percent. Moritzi syndrome is an equally uncommon entity in developed countries with a frequency of one percent to two percent. It is more common in countries with reduced access to medical care occurring with a frequency of four point seven percent to five point seven percent. We believe that this is the first reported case of this extremely uncommon combination of very uncommon diseases.
